full_text: <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I've shared with you all in the past that I have a challenging relationship with time.<|8.32|> <|9.66|> In fact, when I first confessed that in a sermon several years ago, it was actually All Saints Day in 2018, if anyone can remember 2018,<|21.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Several of you remarked that you felt both seen and hopeful by what I had read,<|9.00|> <|9.00|> this assertion that we late people are simply suffering from bouts of optimism.<|17.00|> <|17.00|> Of course we can do one more thing before we go.<|21.00|> <|21.00|> That won't make us late.<|23.70|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So, the very next day, that Monday, I went into my office right after preaching that<|7.12|> <|7.12|> sermon and found that my clock had fallen off the wall, the glass had shattered, and<|17.50|> <|17.50|> the battery had popped out so that literally time had stopped.<|23.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As you can imagine, I was stunned and not just a little spooked.<|7.16|> <|7.16|> And then I remembered that we had just fallen back, and so I had changed my clock in anticipation<|13.76|> <|13.76|> of that.<|14.76|> <|14.76|> And apparently, I hadn't done a very good job rehanging my clock.<|20.00|> <|20.00|> So I discovered I am not only time-challenged, but I'm not very handy.<|25.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> But I think this inability, I'm going to go with more positive spin here, folks, this<|9.28|> <|9.28|> inability to hold time tightly actually helps me live into my life as a Christian.<|18.60|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> As Christians, we must embrace God's kairos time rather than the world's kronos time.<|8.46|> <|8.90|> As Christians, we live in a space where time is slippery and intangible as that incense.<|17.66|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|1.14|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|1.02|> As Christians, we accept that we live simultaneously in a world of now and not yet,<|8.00|> <|8.96|> a collision of all that was and is and is yet to come.<|13.98|> <|15.54|> As Neil Alexander described it when he was with us a few weeks ago,<|19.86|> <|19.86|> our present is animated by all that has been and all that will be.<|28.10|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> we Christians have to hold time lightly.<|4.38|> <|5.34|> We repeat the liturgical cycles and seasons each year,<|10.94|> <|11.22|> and we are constantly reliving the stories of our faith.<|15.46|> <|16.38|> But each year, we are living them through the lens<|20.74|> <|20.74|> of our present moment and current experience.<|25.18|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Sometimes we bury the dead one day and we celebrate baptisms the next,<|6.00|> <|6.54|> again and again inverting logical time and sequence.<|11.80|> <|14.00|> In our life of faith, more than any other place in our lives,<|20.58|> <|21.10|> we are asked to suspend our need for the linear and chronological march of time.<|28.84|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And so here we are, starting a new year at the end of November, not on January 1st, starting<|10.34|> <|10.34|> a new journey with Luke for the next 12 months.<|15.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> And where do we start on this first Sunday of Advent?<|5.86|> <|6.86|> Not with the angel's visit to Mary, announcing her choice as God-bearer.<|12.30|> <|13.00|> Not with Mary and Elizabeth meeting and having the babies within their growing bellies leap in recognition of one another.<|21.14|> <|21.94|> Nope.<|22.30|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> We start this season leading to the birth of the Christ child with a gospel lesson from nearly the end of Luke and some pretty scary images of end times.<|13.18|> <|15.32|> It's understandable if you would come to church at this time of year and expect to hear stories of the coming of a helpless baby who is to be born in a stable weeks from now.<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> but instead by contrast<|3.26|> <|3.26|> we hear this morning of the son of man<|6.82|> <|6.82|> coming in a cloud with power and glory<|10.56|> <|10.56|> and we'll spend the next few weeks of advent<|14.96|> <|14.96|> next couple of weeks<|16.14|> <|16.14|> with John the Baptist shouting about repentance<|20.02|> <|20.02|> and the wilderness<|20.94|> <|20.94|> and all of that happens before today's reading<|26.22|> <|26.22|> and all of that happens before today's reading<|26.50|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> before Jesus' adult ministry begins.<|3.62|> <|4.42|> It's not until the final Sunday of Advent<|8.00|> <|8.00|> that we will hear of Mary and Elizabeth<|10.34|> <|10.34|> and the stirring words of the Magnificat.<|13.70|> <|15.08|> Advent tells us the story backwards.<|18.86|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Doesn't it sort of feel like those lectionary folks got together and they said, "So do<|6.84|> <|6.84|> we give them the good news or the bad news first?"<|11.62|> <|11.62|> And by committee, they decided the bad news.<|16.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Advent reminds us that time for Christians is not linear.<|8.12|> <|9.12|> As one commentator so aptly observed,<|12.80|> <|13.66|> the end precedes its beginning just as the beginning unfurls with the end already in sight.<|21.38|> <|22.00|> How's that for a riddle?<|23.02|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Thank you.<|1.06|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So I wonder sometimes when I hear Episcopalians, you good people out here, talk about how much you love Advent.<|10.54|> <|11.56|> Do you really?<|12.30|> <|13.76|> Do you love these grim warnings of distress among nations, the fainting from fear and foreboding?<|21.34|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Do you love the admonitions to be on guard, be alert, and in the weeks to come to repent?<|7.68|> <|8.82|> Do you really love Advent?<|11.38|> <|13.02|> I think I really love Advent.<|15.56|> <|16.24|> I'm not sure I'm a fan of these dire warnings, but I think I like what the warnings stir up in me.<|24.74|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For I am reminded that when Christmas does finally arrive, it's about so much more than an innocent baby.<|9.84|> <|10.48|> I'm reminded that as I hear the story of that baby, I am hearing it already knowing about that baby's future on a cross.<|21.94|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm reminded that the story of my faith is expressed through time where everything is<|8.54|> <|8.54|> present at once, past, present, and future.<|15.72|> <|15.72|> We are even more acutely aware of this suspended time after these past two years, aren't we?<|24.20|> <|24.20|> Time has felt like it means nothing.<|27.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I struggle to locate myself or events on a clear timeline from March 2020 until now.<|8.40|> <|9.86|> The past two years have been an object lesson in holding time loosely.<|15.62|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> family.<|1.00|><|endoftext|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So how will you spend this Advent season? Is it Advent really, or is it just pre-Christmas?<|11.00|> <|12.08|> Or is it when we actively engage with the whole story of our faith?<|17.58|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Even the bits that make us uncomfortable.<|3.40|> <|4.18|> The bits that talk of being prepared and getting ourselves right with God.<|9.22|> <|9.86|> The bits that remind us of what withstands the vagaries of Kronos time.<|16.50|> <|17.70|> Heaven and earth will pass away.<|19.94|> <|21.54|> Jesus reminds us, but my words will not pass away.<|26.20|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The hope and the promise of another journey through Advent is that no matter what comes,<|8.62|> <|9.54|> distress, confusion, fear, foreboding, a pandemic, God endures.<|18.52|> <|19.30|> The story of our faith endures.<|22.64|> <|23.52|> The past, the present, and the future endures.<|27.40|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> are all present at once as we journey together to Bethlehem.<|6.06|> <|7.16|> Amen.<|7.48|> <|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.56|><|endoftext|>

<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So I wonder sometimes when I hear Episcopalians, you good people out here, talk about how much you love Advent.<|10.54|>
<|13.76|> Do you love these grim warnings of distress among nations, the fainting from fear and foreboding?<|21.34|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Do you love the admonitions to be on guard, be alert, and in the weeks to come to repent?<|7.68|>
<|8.82|> Do you really love Advent?<|11.38|>
<|13.02|> I think I really love Advent.<|15.56|>
<|16.24|> I'm not sure I'm a fan of these dire warnings, but I think I like what the warnings stir up in me.<|24.74|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> For I am reminded that when Christmas does finally arrive, it's about so much more than an innocent baby.<|9.84|>
<|10.48|> I'm reminded that as I hear the story of that baby, I am hearing it already knowing about that baby's future on a cross.<|21.94|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I'm reminded that the story of my faith is expressed through time where everything is<|8.54|>
<|8.54|> present at once, past, present, and future.<|15.72|>
<|15.72|> We are even more acutely aware of this suspended time after these past two years, aren't we?<|24.20|>
<|24.20|> Time has felt like it means nothing.<|27.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> I struggle to locate myself or events on a clear timeline from March 2020 until now.<|8.40|>
<|9.86|> The past two years have been an object lesson in holding time loosely.<|15.62|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> family.<|1.00|><|endoftext|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> So how will you spend this Advent season? Is it Advent really, or is it just pre-Christmas?<|11.00|>
<|12.08|> Or is it when we actively engage with the whole story of our faith?<|17.58|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Even the bits that make us uncomfortable.<|3.40|>
<|4.18|> The bits that talk of being prepared and getting ourselves right with God.<|9.22|>
<|9.86|> The bits that remind us of what withstands the vagaries of Kronos time.<|16.50|>
<|17.70|> Heaven and earth will pass away.<|19.94|>
<|21.54|> Jesus reminds us, but my words will not pass away.<|26.20|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> The hope and the promise of another journey through Advent is that no matter what comes,<|8.62|>
<|9.54|> distress, confusion, fear, foreboding, a pandemic, God endures.<|18.52|>
<|19.30|> The story of our faith endures.<|22.64|>
<|23.52|> The past, the present, and the future endures.<|27.40|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> are all present at once as we journey together to Bethlehem.<|6.06|>
<|startoftranscript|><|en|><|transcribe|><|0.00|> Amen.<|0.56|><|endoftext|>
